title: Apply: The Fourteenth Amendment—Equal Protection
description: Apply your knowledge of how minority groups have gained protections under the Equal Protection Clause in this set of your multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Fourteenth Amendment: Citizenship, Freedom, and Equality

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954).**

We conclude that in the field of public education the doctrine of “separate but equal” has no place. Separate educational facilities are [[☃ definition 1]] unequal. Therefore, we hold that the plaintiffs and others similarly situated for whom the actions have been brought are, by reason of the segregation complained of, deprived of [equal protection of the laws].

**Which statement best summarizes the argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Segregation leads to schools of differing quality and therefore violates the Equal Protection Clause.
Separate facilities are not necessarily unequal, which means they do not violate the Equal Protection Clause.
Segregation is allowable only if separate facilities are divided based on gender or class, not race.
Separate facilities based on gender are unequal and need to be outlawed because they violate equal protection under the law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954).**

We conclude that in the field of public education the doctrine of “separate but equal” has no place. Separate educational facilities are [[☃ definition 1]] unequal. Therefore, we hold that the plaintiffs and others similarly situated for whom the actions have been brought are, by reason of the segregation complained of, deprived of [equal protection of the laws].

**Which Supreme Court case does the Court overturn in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873)
*Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896)
*United States v. Wong Kim Ark* (1898)
*Lochner v. New York* (1905)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Senator Jacob Howard’s speech introducing the Fourteenth Amendment to the Senate in 1866.**

The last two clauses of the first section of the amendment [[☃ definition 1]] a State from depriving not merely a citizen of the United States, but any person, whoever he may be, of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, or from denying to him the equal protection of the laws of the State. This [[☃ definition 2]] all class legislation in the States and does away with the injustice of subjecting one [[☃ definition 3]] of persons to a code not applicable to another. . . . It will, if adopted by the States, forever disable every one of them from passing laws [[☃ definition 4]] upon those fundamental rights and privileges which pertain to citizens of the United States, and to all persons who may happen to be within their jurisdiction. 

**Which statement best summarizes Howard’s argument about the last two clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They guarantee that states can object to federal laws without following regular legal procedures, if those laws take away anyone’s life, liberty, or property.
They prevent citizens from challenging legislation based on class, caste, or status.
They prevent states from passing any laws that run counter to federal laws.
They ensure that states protect the basic rights of all citizens and don’t make unfair laws that treat different groups of people differently.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s majority opinion in *Loving v. Virginia* (1967).**

There can be no question but that Virginia’s [[☃ definition 1]] statutes rest solely upon distinctions drawn according to race. The statutes [[☃ definition 2]] generally accepted conduct if engaged in by members of different races. Over the years, this Court has consistently [[☃ definition 3]] “(d)istinctions between citizens solely because of their ancestry” as being “[[☃ definition 4]] to a free people whose institutions are founded upon the doctrine of equality.” At the very least, the Equal Protection Clause demands that racial classifications, especially suspect in criminal statutes, be subjected to the ‘most rigid scrutiny,’ and, if they are ever to be upheld, they must be shown to be necessary to the accomplishment of some permissible state objective, independent of the racial discrimination which it was the object of the Fourteenth Amendment to eliminate. 

**Which statement best expresses the argument in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a now offensive term for interracial relationships

choices.content: Virginia’s laws violate the Equal Protection Clause because they limit the right to marry to White couples only.
Virginia’s laws against mixed-race marriages do not violate the Equal Protection Clause, as states have the power to regulate marriage.
Virginia’s laws against interracial relationships treat people differently based on their race, and the Court has long rejected such laws.
Virginia’s laws give state governments too much power over the right to marry, which is a right regulated by the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873).**

We agree with [Bradwell’s lawyer] that there are privileges and immunities belonging to citizens of the United States, in that relation and character, and that it is these and these alone which a state is forbidden to [[☃ definition 1]]. But the right to admission to practice in the courts of a state is not one of them. This right in no sense depends on citizenship of the United States. It has not, as far as we know, ever been made in any state, or in any case, to depend on citizenship at all. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in this excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to practice law in state courts is not one of the rights guaranteed to citizens under the Constitution.
The right to practice law is an unenumerated right that all people have and cannot be taken away by state governments.
The right to practice law can be restricted by the federal government but not by state governments.
The right to practice law is an enumerated right listed in the Constitution, so it must be protected.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s opinion in *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873).**

We agree with [Bradwell’s lawyer] that there are privileges and immunities belonging to citizens of the United States, in that relation and character, and that it is these and these alone which a state is forbidden to [[☃ definition 1]]. But the right to admission to practice in the courts of a state is not one of them. This right in no sense depends on citizenship of the United States. It has not, as far as we know, ever been made in any state, or in any case, to depend on citizenship at all. 

**Which statement best describes the relationship between the reasoning in the excerpt and the ruling in *United States v. Virginia Military Institute (VMI)*?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both cases ruled that the Equal Protection Clause could not be used to challenge gender discrimination.
*Bradwell* failed to combat discrimination based on sex, whereas *VMI* struck down a male-only admissions policy using the Equal Protection Clause.
Both cases ruled that the states had violated the Equal Protection Clause by allowing discrimination based on sex.
*Bradwell* held that sex-based discrimination was never allowed under the Equal Protection Clause, whereas *VMI* provided legal exceptions to this rule.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from New Jersey Representative Andrew Rogers’s comments in the records of debate in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

Why, sir, all the rights we have under the laws of the country are embraced under the definition of privileges and immunities. The right to vote is a privilege. The right to marry is a privilege. The right to contract is a privilege. The right to be a juror is a privilege. . . . I hold if [Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment] ever becomes a part of the fundamental law of the land it will prevent any State from refusing to allow anything to anybody embraced under this term of privileges and immunities. If a [[☃ definition 1]] is refused the right to be a juror, that will take away from  him his privileges and immunities as a citizen of the United States, and the Federal Government will step in and interfere, the result will be a contest between the powers of the Federal Government and the powers of the States. It will result in a revolution worse than that through which we have just passed.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an outdated term for a Black person; now considered offensive

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment does not include enough details about what privileges and immunities are protected by the Constitution.
The Fourteenth Amendment does not give the federal government the power to enforce Section 1 in the states, making it useless.
The Fourteenth Amendment’s protection of privileges and immunities only applies at the federal level and not at the state level of government.
The Fourteenth Amendment’s protection of privileges and immunities is too broad and conflicts with what the states are willing to allow.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from New Jersey Representative Andrew Rogers’s comments in the records of debate in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

Why, sir, all the rights we have under the laws of the country are embraced under the definition of privileges and immunities. The right to vote is a privilege. The right to marry is a privilege. The right to contract is a privilege. The right to be a juror is a privilege. . . . I hold if [Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment] ever becomes a part of the fundamental law of the land it will prevent any State from refusing to allow anything to anybody embraced under this term of privileges and immunities. If a [[☃ definition 1]] is refused the right to be a juror, that will take away from  him his privileges and immunities as a citizen of the United States, and the Federal Government will step in and interfere, the result will be a contest between the powers of the Federal Government and the powers of the States. It will result in a revolution worse than that through which we have just passed.

**The argument in the excerpt most directly contrasts with the reasoning in which Supreme Court case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an outdated term for a Black person; now considered offensive

choices.content: *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873)
*Loving v. Virginia (1967)
*Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015)
*Bostock v. Clayton* (2020)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896), written by Justice Henry B. Brown.**

The object of the [Fourteenth] amendment was undoubtedly to enforce the absolute equality of the two races before the law, but, in the nature of things, it could not have been intended to [[☃ definition 1]] distinctions based upon color, or to enforce social, as distinguished from political, equality, or a [[☃ definition 2]] of the two races upon terms unsatisfactory to either.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fourteenth Amendment prevents discrimination only when one race is significantly harmed, not when one race benefits from particular actions.
The Fourteenth Amendment defines only two races in the United States, White and Black, and cannot be applied to other races.
The Fourteenth Amendment does not force states to uphold equality in all situations, but only to uphold civil equality under the law.
The Fourteenth Amendment states that any form of discrimination based on race is unconstitutional.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896), written by Justice Henry B. Brown.**

The object of the [Fourteenth] amendment was undoubtedly to enforce the absolute equality of the two races before the law, but, in the nature of things, it could not have been intended to [[☃ definition 1]] distinctions based upon color, or to enforce social, as distinguished from political, equality, or a [[☃ definition 2]] of the two races upon terms unsatisfactory to either.

**The argument in the excerpt was later overturned in which Supreme Court case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873)
*Brown v. Board of Education* (1954)
*Loving v. Virginia* (1967)
*Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard* (2023), written by Chief Justice John Roberts.**

[When] a university admits students “on the basis of race, it engages in the offensive and [[☃ definition 1]] assumption that [students] of a particular race, because of their race, think alike” . . . In doing so, the university furthers “stereotypes that treat individuals as the product of their race, evaluating their thoughts and efforts—their very worth as citizens—according to a criterion barred to the Government by history and the Constitution.”. . . 

For the reasons provided above, the Harvard and UNC admissions programs cannot be [[☃ definition 2]] with the guarantees of the Equal Protection Clause. Both programs lack sufficiently focused and measurable objectives [[☃ definition 3]] the use of race, unavoidably employ race in a negative manner, involve racial stereotyping, and lack meaningful end points. We have never permitted admissions programs to work in that way, and we will not do so today.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Equal Protection Clause allows the use of race-based quotas in admission processes.
The Equal Protection Clause requires universities to consider the racial background of applicants to ensure diversity.
The Equal Protection Clause can be used to create affirmative action policies in admission if doing so supports the advancement of a particular race.
The Equal Protection Clause does not allow university admissions programs that treat students in a stereotypical or harmful way.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from New York Representative James Brooks’s comments in the records of debate in the House of Representatives in 1866.**

I raise my voice here on behalf of 15 million of our countrywomen, the fairest, brightest portion of creation, and I ask why they are not permitted to be represented under this [[☃ definition 3]] . . . Why, in organizing a system of [[☃ definition 1]] and justice, not recognize in the case of free women as well as free [[☃ definition 2]] the right of representation? 

**Which Supreme Court case applied the Fourteenth Amendment in the way that is described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the quality of encouraging political freedom
an outdated term for Black people; now considered offensive

choices.content: *Bradwell v. State of Illinois* (1873)
*Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896)
*United States v. Virginia* (1996)
*Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard* (2023)
